behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior (recursive watch semantics and event delivery from nested paths). A mock watcher can falsely pass even if OS/backend watcher config misses subdirectories. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text explicitly depends on real watch scope in a live daemon ('watches a configured input directory', 'runs continuously').
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory, write a file into it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily cross-module state handoff (event intake -> dedup window store -> pipeline trigger) and configurable timing logic; no real OS/network/subprocess is required to expose the core failure. Rejection gate: System was rejected because behavior text is about dedup policy for repeated requests, not OS-specific event semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire daemon intake + dedup component + processing dispatcher in-process with a controllable clock; submit two requests for the same canonical path within the configured window and assert only one dispatch occurs, then submit after window expiry and assert dispatch resumes.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly names OS-emitted duplicate events; real failure appears in watcher backend/event coalescing under actual filesystem notifications. Mocked emitters can miss true duplicate patterns and timing. Rejection gate: Integration rejected by explicit text 'operating system emits multiple filesystem events'.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory and perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write + rename or append bursts). Capture processing records and assert exactly one processing run for the target file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Requires real OS filesystem lifecycle behavior (directory deletion/recreation invalidating watch handles). In-process fakes typically do not reproduce watcher teardown/rebind edge cases. Rejection gate: Integration rejected because behavior explicitly requires remove/recreate while running, which is OS-level watch handle behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, delete the watched directory while running, recreate it at the same path, then create a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is ordering logic across modules (metadata read -> queue sort -> dispatcher). This can be caught with real local filesystem fixtures or injected metadata in-process; no subprocess/network/LLM needed. Rejection gate: System considered, but behavior text does not require OS-specific watcher semantics beyond obtaining timestamps; ordering policy itself is module-level orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: In an integration harness, submit multiple file-arrival requests with controlled creation timestamps (or create files and read their timestamps through the same metadata module), then assert processing order matches ascending creation time.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem timing/locking/race condition between write completion and event handling. Mocking file writes/events can hide partial-write visibility failures. Rejection gate: Integration rejected because behavior explicitly depends on write-in-progress state at event time, an OS/filesystem runtime condition.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real watched directory, open a file and write in chunks with delays while keeping the write stream open; verify processing does not start until stream close/fsync completion, then verify exactly one completed-output artifact is produced.
